今天，世界各地的执法机构联盟宣布了一项名为“破坏者”的协调行动的结果，该行动针对黑暗网络上非法商品的卖家和买家。
这次行动是在去年5月华尔街市场被取缔之后进行的，华尔街市场当时是世界上第二大非法网络市场。这次行动由德国联邦刑事警察(Bundeskriminalamt)牵头，荷兰国家警察(Politie)欧洲刑警组织、欧洲司法组织和美国多个政府机构支持，为调查人员提供了量化数据和材料，以确定用于非法活动的黑暗网络账户背后的嫌疑人。
结果，在欧洲和美国各地，179名从事数万次非法商品销售的小贩被逮捕。警方检获超过650万元现金和虚拟货币，以及约500公斤毒品，包括芬太尼、羟考酮、氢可酮、甲基苯丙胺、海洛因、可卡因、摇头丸、摇头丸和含有成瘾物质的药物；以及64支枪支。
逮捕行动在美国(121人)、德国(42人)、荷兰(8人)、英国(4人)、奥地利(3人)和瑞典(1人)进行。一些调查仍在进行中，以查明黑暗网络账户背后的个人。
“破坏者行动”由欧洲刑警组织和欧洲司法组织协调的一系列独立但相辅相成的联合行动组成，所有这些行动的目的都是对暗网上非法货物的销售产生全球影响。
这次行动是奥地利、塞浦路斯、德国、荷兰、瑞典、澳大利亚、加拿大、联合王国和美国执法和司法当局共同努力的结果。
欧洲刑警组织欧洲网络犯罪中心负责人EdvardasŠileris在评论这次行动时说：
执法在一起最有效，今天的公告向在黑暗网络上出售或购买非法商品的犯罪分子发出了一个强烈的信息：隐藏的互联网不再隐藏，你的匿名活动也不是匿名的。执法部门致力于追捕罪犯，无论他们在哪里行动-无论是在街上还是在电脑屏幕后面。
黑暗网络市场的黄金时代已经结束。这样的行动突显了执法部门打击黑暗网络市场的加密和匿名性的能力。警方不再只取缔这些非法市场-他们还追捕通过这些网站买卖非法商品的犯罪分子。
将你的生命置于危险之中：危险的非法药物，如芬太尼或假冒药物可能会杀死你。
执法部门还可以将非法交易追溯到买家和卖家。从隐蔽地点购买非法货物的个人在一些国家面临被起诉的风险。
黑暗的网络不再是童话故事--卖家和买家不再隐藏在阴影中。
塞浦路斯：塞浦路斯警方(ΑστυνομίαΚύπρου)。
德国：联邦刑事警察办公室(Bundeskriminalamt)、州刑事警察办公室(Landeskrialämter)、地方主管警察部队和海关调查办公室(Zollfahndungsämter)。
美国：司法部(DoJ)、联邦调查局(FBI)、移民和海关执法局-国土安全调查(HSI)、缉毒管理局(DEA)、美国邮政检查局(USPIS)、海关和边境保护局(CBP)、金融犯罪执法网(FinCEN)、烟酒火器局(ATF)、海军刑事调查局(NCIS)和国防部(DOD)
note_en:
Today, a coalition of law enforcement agencies across the world announced the results of a coordinated operation known as DisrupTor which targeted vendors and buyers of illicit goods on the dark web.
 This operation follows the takedown in May of last year of  Wall Street Market, the world’s then second largest illegal online market in the dark web. Led by the German Federal Criminal Police (Bundeskriminalamt) with the support of the Dutch National Police (Politie) Europol,  Eurojust and various US government agencies, this takedown provided investigators with quantitative data and materials to identify suspects behind dark web accounts used for illegal activity.
 As a result, 179 vendors who engaged in tens of thousands of sales of illicit good were arrested across Europe and the United States. Over $6.5 million were seized in both cash and virtual currencies, alongside some 500 kilograms of drugs, including fentanyl, oxycodone, hydrocodone, methamphetamine, heroin, cocaine, ecstasy, MDMA, and medicine containing addictive substances; and 64 firearms.
 The arrests were carried out in the United States (121), Germany (42), the Netherlands (8), United Kingdom (4), Austria (3), and Sweden (1). A number of investigations are still ongoing to identify the individuals behind dark web accounts.
  Operation DisrupTor was composed of a series of separate but complementary joint operations coordinated by Europol and Eurojust, all aimed at making a global impact on the sale of illicit goods on the dark web.
 This operation was the result of a collaborative effort between the law enforcement and judicial authorities of Austria, Cyprus, Germany, the Netherlands, Sweden, Australia, Canada, the United Kingdom and the United States.
 Commenting on this operation, the Head of  Europol’s European Cybercrime Centre (EC3),  Edvardas Šileris, said:
 Law enforcement is most effective when working together, and today’s announcement sends a strong message to criminals selling or buying illicit goods on the dark web: the hidden internet is no longer hidden, and your anonymous activity is not anonymous. Law enforcement is committed to tracking down criminals, no matter where they operate – be it on the streets or behind a computer screen.
  The golden age of dark web marketplace is over. Operations such as these highlight the capability of law enforcement to counter encryption and anonymity of dark web market places. Police no longer only takes down such illegal marketplaces – they also chase down the criminals buying and selling illegal goods through such sites.
    Putting your life in danger: dangerous illegal drugs such as fentanyl or counterfeit substances could kill you.
 Law enforcement can also trace back illicit transactions to both the buyer and seller. An individual who purchased illicit goods from hidden sites is at risk of prosecution in a number of countries.
 The dark web is not a fairy tale - vendors and buyers are no longer hidden in the shadow.
 Cyprus: Cyprus Police (Αστυνομία Κύπρου)
 Germany: Federal Criminal Police Office (Bundeskriminalamt), State Criminal Police Offices (Landeskriminalämter), the competent local police forces and Customs Investigation Offices (Zollfahndungsämter)
      United States: Department of Justice (DOJ), Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI), Immigration and Customs Enforcement – Homeland Security Investigations (HSI), Drug Enforcement Administration (DEA), US Postal Inspection Service (USPIS), Customs and Border Protection (CBP), Financial Crimes Enforcement Network (FinCEN), Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, and Firearms (ATF), Naval Criminal Investigative Service (NCIS) and Department of Defense (DOD)
title: 国际打击暗网供应商导致179人被捕
title_en: International Sting Against Dark Web Vendors Leads to 179 Arrests

note:
Write is…。硬的。像大多数有抱负的博客作者一样，我的草稿文件夹和我对未来多产的梦想超过了我的实际产出。
不过，我发现了一个克服这一障碍的奇妙窍门：写推特帖子。我已经发布了很多关于我感兴趣的话题的小推文：
这些正是我想要在博客上谈论的事情！但不知何故，我发现发布推特帖子要容易10倍。
我已经能听到你的呻吟了。当然，发推特比写要容易，你这个笨蛋！当我们用警句和模因取代深刻的逻辑思维时，我们的头脑正被一次280个字符驱赶进绞肉机。推特是一种用类固醇思考的PPT。
但我认为这种不屑一顾的反应没有抓住要点。如果把推特当作文章和研究论文的平庸替代品，我们就不能真正理解它。我们需要将其视为一种新的媒介，以其自身的方式。特别值得一提的是，Twitter是一种素描的媒介--可以随时玩弄想法。推特更像是在白板上涂鸦，或者在咖啡馆里乱扔想法，而不是写一本书。(我所说的素描并不是字面意义上的素描；我指的是任何轻量级的早期思想表达。)。
正确的约束：好的绘制工具为您可以执行的操作提供了正确的限制。Twitter的限制超出了显而易见的字符数。
门槛低：Twitter让入门变得很容易。但是，最重要的是，它也让它更容易完成！
社交背景：Twitter为思考提供了一个高度相互关联的背景。我们应该担心这做得太过分了吗？
通过反思这些属性，我认为我们不仅可以对Twiter有一些具体的了解，而且可以对思考工具的更广泛的前景有一些了解。我们跳下去吧。
思考大的新事物是很困难的，我们的大脑善于通过找到一些更容易做的事情来逃避工作，但仍然看起来很有效率。不幸的是，在勾勒出一个想法的早期阶段，这样的事情比比皆是：担心最后一段的选词而不是写下一段，纠结于字体大小，创建一个新的博客系统，而不是写该死的博客帖子。
我们可以尝试避免这些诱惑，但更简单的方法是简单地找到一开始就不允许这些诱惑的工具。这是好的绘制工具的一个关键属性：它们提供正确的约束。
从最明显的一个开始：280个字符的限制。推特的主要限制是鼓励简洁。当你的工作空间如此有限的时候，很难纠结于用词的选择上。Twitter的对话语气在这方面也有帮助-我可以像说话一样写作，任何花哨的词语都会显得不合时宜。当然，我不能调整字体和页边距，这会切断分心矢量。
但是，这些线索让人物限制的故事变得有点复杂。真正的限制并不是你的整个观点必须放在一条推文中，而是你的每一个单独的观点都必须挤在这个限制之下。这提供了一个不同的有用约束：每个想法都必须包装在一个小小的原子包中。我发现这有助于理清我的思想之间的界限，并澄清论点的离散单位。
这种约束有点类似于大纲工具的好处。但是Twitter还有另一个限制：线程是线性的！不允许缩进。这迫使论点之间有一条轻快的直线，而不是陷入第一个想法的次要论点的细微之处。非常有限，但同时也是自由的。
综上所述，这些约束确定了介质的利弊，以及其适当的使用范围。显然，用单一层次的大纲写一本书是愚蠢的，但它适用于粗略的草图。更有趣的是，我认为Twitter用来说服持怀疑态度的读者是无用的；根本没有空间提供足够的细节和背景。这是媒体素描的一个共同属性：最初的模型不足以影响用户，即使它对内部团队来说是一个有用的工具。我更喜欢将Twitter作为一种与同情的各方讨论想法的方式，他们已经有了足够的背景来分享我对这些想法的兴奋。
也许这里有一个普遍的原则：Twitter有利于勾勒想法，原因与它不利于充分开发想法的原因是一样的。你不能无意中开始在Twitter上写书，这就是问题的关键。
一般来说，草图工具的正确约束是什么？我认为这个问题比乍看起来更深。
你可能会说“只提供传达观点所需的最低保真度”，但我认为如何定义这个最低水平并不明显。用肥大的记号笔画素描可以防止我们的画过于详细；我最近在iPad素描中使用的缪斯(Muse)故意将你的墨水选择限制在几种颜色上。
这对某些类型的思考和模型非常有效。但是为了设计新的动画和物理交互，我们需要一种完全不同的具有更多功能的工具！本质和虚假之间的界限取决于目标。
提供正确的约束并不总是要移除的问题。它可能还需要添加高级功能，比如这个字体设计工具，它使用花哨的机器学习来提供几个简单的旋钮来控制诸如“粗体”之类的东西。它不允许您移动单个矢量点，而是允许您在更自然的抽象级别上操作。
如果你不小心，约束很容易损害流动性-正如恩格尔巴特所展示的那样，把一块砖绑在铅笔上不会产生一个高效的工具。
总体而言，我们似乎想要一些约束来帮助我们保持流畅思维的轨道，但不排除太多有趣的可能性。同时考虑这两个标准是一个微妙的平衡行动，我看不到容易的答案。
在Twitter上起步的门槛很低。只需点击一个按钮，输入一个想法，不需要花费一分钟来记住如何启动我的博客服务器。通常，第一分钟的摩擦足以阻止我进入写作的流程。
但更有趣的现象是完成的门槛很低。在推特上，一句话是完全可以接受的出版单位。超过这一点的任何事情都是一种额外的奖励。相比之下，我的大部分博客帖子都没有发表，因为我担心它们不完整，或者在某些方面不够好。这些未发表的草稿显然比一条推特要完整得多，但因为它们在博客上，它们不会感觉到“完成了”，很难克服分享的恐惧。
这似乎是素描工具的关键部分：当你绘制草图时，应该明白你的想法是不成熟的，在这种状态下分享它是安全的。对于经过打磨、彻底的人工制品…是有时间和地点的。这不是推特！大家都知道你刚画了个速写。
我相信数量决定质量。陶艺课上制作更多陶罐的学生比痴迷于制作单一完美陶罐的学生进步更快。一个内置了低完成门槛的工具可以更容易地克服恐惧，做更多的工作，并在更早的阶段分享它。
根据我的经验，素描总是需要在个人思考和合作之间进行微妙的舞蹈。你画草图是为了给自己澄清一些事情，也是为了与他人交流。我认为一个好的素描媒介应该考虑到这个过程的两个方面。
写博客可能感觉就像一面孤独的单向镜子：向世界发布一些东西，可能会得到一些回复和一些黑客新闻的尖刻评论。相比之下，推特是一个热闹的集市。接合率是完全不同的。你可以很容易地围绕一条线索中的各个点进行微对话。当同样的人开始一次又一次地出现时，感觉就像在村子里看到熟人一样。在Twitter上，我为我的Twitter朋友写作，而不是为一些杂乱无章的人群写作。
在最好的情况下，这种约定导致了我最喜欢的素描课程的特点是来回穿梭。想法就在空气中，不清楚它们到底是从哪里来的，它们会实时地结合在一起形成新的想法。对我来说，Twitter在模拟白板会议的激动人心的创造性能量方面做得出奇地好。人们突然出现在谈话中，提供洞察力；树和子树形成了对先前观点的模仿。
当然，我在这里的安全感假定来自其他各方的健康参与，这是一种并不是所有人都享有的特权。我想，这样一个全球性的公共媒体完全适合素描，这似乎有些奇怪--这似乎只是因为我找到了安全、信任的迷你社区，由非正式和可渗透的边界定义。也许一个更私密的推特更适合素描，尽管它可能会让新的人无法进入对话？
为了尝试在博客媒体上勾勒出更多，我只是在几个小时内匆匆写出了这篇博客帖子，所以我并没有真正得出一个宏伟的结论。然而，我仍然在点击发布！
我很好奇地想更多地思考不同类型的创意工具所提供的约束/自由，以及我们是否可以更聪明地利用这些约束来实现新类型的草图。我对各种素描特别好奇，这些素描只有在电脑的帮助下才能完成，而纸和笔是做不到的。纸和笔是很棒的工具，但是外面还有什么呢？
我想我会试着在博客上更自由地描述这样的想法。如果你想看我未来关于这类东西的文章，请点击下面的订阅链接之一。如果你有想法要分享，给我发一封电子邮件或推特！
如果你喜欢这篇文章，这里有一些人比我在这里更深入、更有说服力地写/讲了相关主题：
我定期撰写关于编程工具、最终用户编程和其他软件主题的文章。要获取有关新帖子的更新，请执行以下操作：
note_en:
Writing is…hard. Like most aspiring bloggers, my folders of drafts and my dreams of future prolificness outweigh my actual output.
 I’ve found a curious trick for getting over this hurdle, though: writing tweet threads. I’ve published many little bursts of tweets about topics I’m curious about:
  These are exactly the kinds of things I’d like to blog about! But somehow, I’ve found it 10 times easier to publish the tweet threads.
 I can hear you groaning already. Of course tweeting is easier than writing, you dummy! Our minds are being driven into the meat grinder 280 characters at a time, as we replace deep logical thought with aphorisms and memes. Twitter is PowerPoint thinking on steroids.
 But I think this dismissive response misses the point. We can’t really understand Twitter by treating it as a mediocre replacement for essays and research papers. We need to see it as a new medium on its own terms. In particular,  Twitter is a medium for  sketching—for playing with ideas, on the fly. Twitter is more similar to scribbling on a whiteboard or tossing ideas around at the cafe than writing a book. (By sketching I don’t mean literally just drawing; I mean any lightweight early expression of a thought.)
  The right constraints: Good sketching tools provide the right limits on what you can do. Twitter’s constraints go beyond the obvious character count.
 Low barriers: Twitter makes it easy to get started. But, crucially, it also makes it easy to  finish!
 A social context: Twitter provides a highly interconnected context for thinking. Should we be worried it takes it too far?
 By reflecting on these properties, I think we can gain some insight not just into Twiter specifically, but also the broader landscape of tools for thinking. Let’s dive in.
  Thinking about big new things is hard, and our brains are good at finding ways to weasel out of the job by finding something easier to do, but still plausibly productive. Unfortunately, in the early stages of sketching out an idea, such distractions abound: worrying about word choice in the last paragraph instead of writing the next one, futzing with the font size, making a new blog system instead of writing the damn blog post.
 We can try to avoid these temptations, but an easier route is to simply find tools that don’t allow have the temptations in the first place.  This is a key property of good sketching tools: they provide the right constraints.
   Start with the obvious one:  the 280 character limit. Twitter’s main constraint is encouraging concision. It’s hard to dwell on word choice when you have so little space to work with. Twitter’s conversational tone also helps here—I can just  write like I talk, and any fancy words would seem out of place. And of course, I can’t tweak fonts and margins, which cuts off a distraction vector.
 But threads complicate the story of character limits a bit. The limit isn’t really that your entire point must fit into one tweet—it’s that   each of your individual points must squeeze under the limit. This provides a different useful constraint: each idea has to be wrapped in a little atomic package. I find this helpful for figuring out the boundaries between my thoughts and clarifying the discrete units of an argument.
 That constraint sort of resembles the benefits of an outlining tool. But Twitter has another constraint:  a thread is linear! No indenting allowed. This forces a brisk straightline through the argument, instead of getting mired in the fine points of the sub-sub-sub-arguments of the first idea. Very limiting, but simultaneously freeing.
 Taken together, these constraints frame the pros and cons of the medium, its appropriate range of usage. Obviously, writing a book in a single-level outline would be foolish, but it works for a rough sketch. More interestingly, I think Twitter is useless for persuading a skeptical reader; there’s simply not space for providing enough detail and context. This is a common property of media for sketching: the initial mockup isn’t impressive enough to sway a user, even if it’s a useful tool for the internal team. I prefer to use Twitter as a way to workshop ideas with sympathetic parties who already have enough context to share my excitement about the ideas.
 Perhaps there’s a general principle here: Twitter is good for sketching ideas for the same reasons it’s bad for fully developing them. You can’t accidentally start writing a book in Twitter, and that’s kind of the point.
  In general, what are the  right constraints for a sketching tool? I think this question is deeper than it seems at first glance.
 You might say something like “only offer the minimum fidelity needed to convey the point,” but I think it’s not obvious how to define that minimum level.  Sketching with a fat marker can prevent us from getting too detailed with our drawings;  Muse, which I’ve been using for iPad sketching recently, intentionally limits your ink choices to just a few colors.
 This works great for certain kinds of thinking and mockups. But for designing new interactions with animation and physics, we need  a totally different class of tools with more capabilities! The line between essential and spurious depends on the goal.
 Providing the right constraints isn’t always a matter of removing. It can require adding advanced capabiliites too, like this  typeface design tool that uses fancy machine learning to provide a few simple knobs for controlling things like “bold.” It doesn’t let you move individual vector points, but instead lets you operate at a more natural level of abstraction.
 If you’re not careful, constraints can easily damage fluidity—as  Engelbart showed, tying a brick to a pencil does not yield a productive tool.
  Overall, it seems that we want constraints that help keep us on track with fluid thought, but don’t rule out too many interesting possibilities. Considering both of these criteria together is a subtle balancing act, and I don’t see easy answers.
  There’s a  low barrier to starting on Twitter. Just click a button, type a thought, no need to spend a minute remembering how to start my blog server. Often, that first minute of friction is enough to prevent me from getting into the flow of writing.
 But the more interesting phenomenon is the  low barrier to finishing. On Twitter, a single sentence is a completely acceptable unit of publication. Anything beyond that is sort of a bonus. In contrast, most of my blog posts go unpublished because I fear they’re not complete, or not good enough in some dimension. These unpublished drafts are obviously far more complete than a single tweet, but because they’re on a blog, they don’t feel “done,” and it’s hard to overcome the fear of sharing.
 This seems like a crucial part of sketching tools: when you make a sketch, it should be understood that your idea is immature, and feel safe to share it in that state.  There’s a time and a place for polished, deeply thorough artifacts… and it’s not Twitter! Everyone knows you just did a quick sketch.
 I believe that quantity leads to quality.  The students who make more pots in ceramics class improve faster than the students who obsess over making a single perfect pot. A tool with a built-in low barrier to finishing makes it easier to overcome the fear, do more work, and share it at an earlier stage.
  In my experience, sketching always requires a delicate dance between individual thought and collaboration. You sketch to clarify something for yourself, but also to communicate with others. I think a good sketching medium should account for both halves of the process.
 Writing a blog can feel like a lonely one-way mirror: release something into the world, maybe get a few comments back and some Hacker News snark. In contrast,  Twitter is a bazaar, buzzing with activity. The engagement ratio is totally different. You can easily have micro-conversations around individual points in a thread. When the same people start showing up time after time, it starts to feel like  seeing acquaintances in a village. On Twitter, I write for my Twitter friends, not for some amorphous crowd.
 At its best, this engagement leads to the kind of back-and-forth that characterizes my favorite kinds of sketching sessions. Ideas are in the air, it’s not clear where they came from really, they combine to form new ones in realtime. For me, Twitter does an oddly good job at simulating the thrilling creative energy of a whiteboarding session. People pop in and out of the conversation offering insights; trees and sub-trees form riffing off of earlier points.
 Of course, my feeling of safety here presumes healthy engagement from other parties, a privilege not enjoyed by all. I suppose it’s kind of odd that such a globally public medium is suitable at all for sketching—it seems only possible because I’ve found safe, trusting mini-communities, defined by informal and permeable boundaries. Perhaps a more private Twitter would be even better for sketching, although it might cut out new people from entering the conversation?
  In an attempt to  sketch more in the blog medium, I just whipped up this blog post in a couple hours, so I don’t really have a grand conclusion. And yet I’m still hitting publish!
 I’m curious to think more about the constraints/freedoms afforded by different kinds of creative tools, and whether we could get more clever with those constraints to enable new kinds of sketching. I’m especially curious about kinds of sketching which are only possible thanks to computers, and couldn’t have been done with paper and pen. Paper and pen are great tools, but what else is out there?
 I guess I’ll try to keep sketching more freely on the blog about ideas like this. Click one of the subscribe links below if you’d like to see my future writing on this sort of thing. And if you have thoughts to share,  send me an email or  a tweet!
  If you enjoyed this post, here are some people who have written/spoken about related topics in much greater depth and eloquence than I have here:
 I periodically write about programming tools, end-user programming, and other software topics. To get updates about new posts:
title: 推特帖子解决了我作家的障碍：推特是素描的媒介
title_en: Tweet threads cured my writer's block: Twitter as a medium for sketching

note:
新冠肺炎摧毁了全球经济，迫使世界各国政府投入数万亿美元进行恢复工作。
这场大流行本可以成为与气候变化作斗争的决定性时刻--这是各国领导人拯救环境、推动地球走向更绿色未来的机会。
相反，CNN发现，一些最大的化石燃料生产国正在投入纳税人的钱来扶持污染行业。独家最新数据显示，这些决定让世界离气候灾难又近了一步。
“这是我们唯一的机会，”气候智库新气候研究所(NewClimate Institute)的创始合伙人尼克拉斯·赫恩(Niklas Höhne)教授说，他也是与CNN分享的“气候行动跟踪者”(Climate Action Tracker)即将进行的一项研究的合著者。
研究表明，世界正在远远落后于本已不足的目标，即将全球变暖控制在比工业化前水平高出2摄氏度以下，并努力将其控制在1.5摄氏度以内。
Höhne说：“我们正处于一种花费巨额资金的情况下。”“未来10到20年内，从政府预算中支出如此多资金的类似机会不会出现.”
由于冠状病毒的封锁，全球温室气体排放量已经下降。目前的经济低迷意味着未来的排放量预计会略低。
但根据气候行动跟踪者的独家数据，最近的下降并没有对长期气候变化产生任何真正的影响。
联合国表示，有必要逐步淘汰化石燃料，以阻止灾难性的人为气候变化。但要摆脱它们将是一件困难的事情。在世界各地，社区的能源、工作和生计都依赖于化石燃料。反过来，政府依赖于他们的选票和税收。
下面，我们来看看世界上的四个地区再次转向化石燃料来帮助挽救他们的经济。
波兰正花费3500万美元购买不需要的煤炭，以缓解这个受到疫情冲击的行业。“。在加拿大，阿尔伯塔省正在投资11亿美元修建一条新的输油管道，这条管道被认为是“经济复苏必不可少的”。澳大利亚昆士兰州正在快速建设一座新煤矿，以帮助该州“从新冠肺炎的影响中恢复过来”。印度正在向私营部门开放数十个煤矿，以“化新冠肺炎危机为契机”。
这些社区正在进行气候变化之战，人们的生计依赖于世界其他地区需要他们放弃的行业。
在波兰的这个地区，当地的童子军开展活动以保持镇上煤矿的开放。
土木工程师皮奥特·普平(Piotr Pupin)在过去19年里一直在波兰西南部的图罗(Turów)褐煤矿场工作。就像他的父亲一样，他希望继续在那里工作，直到几十年后退休的时候。
他有麻烦了。虽然露天矿拥有足够的煤炭可以继续运营到2044年，但它目前的许可证将在短短6年内到期。由于气候危机，欧盟内部对进一步延期的反对声音很大。
这位44岁的土木工程师在附近的Zgorzelec镇长大。在弗罗茨瓦夫上大学时，他获得了由当时经营图罗的公司支付的奖学金。作为资金的交换，他签署了一份为期三年的合同，在矿井工作。从那以后，他一直留在这里，一级又一级地晋升到现在的投资主管职位。他的团队监督对矿山内所有基础设施的投资，从道路到建筑和电气设备，再到现场漫游的巨型机械。
“我本来没打算待这么久，但事情就这样发生了。我的职业生涯正在进步，一切都很顺利，“他说。“这不是我梦寐以求的工作，但它给了我舒适、稳定和安全。”
在这个地区，大多数人要么在矿山综合体工作，要么在边境另一边工作。
由同一家公司运营的煤矿及其附属发电厂的潜在关闭，对普平和其他数千人来说将是灾难性的。
“我得找一份新工作。最有可能的是，那将意味着要跨越国界，“他说。他的技能并不局限于采矿，但他怀疑该地区是否会有工作给他。
“这一地区的所有建筑公司，那些我有资历可以为之工作的公司，都在矿场工作。当矿厂关闭时，这些公司就不会有光明的前景了。“。
普平知道矿场最终会关闭。波兰对煤炭的需求正在减少，取而代之的是天然气和可再生能源。图罗已经感受到了影响。他说：“每年，矿场的工人人数都会下降。人们退休了，不会雇佣新人来接替他们，“他说。他知道他的两个女儿长大后很可能会搬走。该地区对矿山的依赖程度如此之高，很难找到一份与矿场无关的好工作。
但他希望能留任到最后。“我的两个姐妹离开波兰去了爱尔兰。这就够了。“他说。
图罗的长期未来非常不确定。该矿场位于波兰附录般的小尖端，坐落在德国和捷克之间，在那里面临着环境和气候活动人士以及居民的强烈反对。这两个国家都请愿反对该煤矿，尽管它们也依赖煤炭发电。根据国际能源署(International Energy Agency)的数据，波兰80%的国内能源来自煤炭，而捷克和德国的这一比例分别为54%和43%。
褐煤，有时因其颜色而被称为褐煤，是效率最低、污染最严重的煤炭类型。与地下开采的地质较老的硬煤相比，它的燃料价值更低，排放强度更高。褐煤很难运输，所以通常在开采的同一地点燃烧以获取能源。在图罗，新开采的煤炭通过传送带运送到大约3公里外的一家发电厂。
波兰政府表示，图罗在国家能源安全中扮演着战略角色。据运营该矿的国有控股公司PGE称，它为大约230万波兰家庭供电。PGE现在正计划将挖掘范围扩大到更靠近捷克边境的地方，这一计划导致了这两个长期盟友之间的紧张关系。
捷克已经向欧盟提出申诉，辩称波兰在3月份单方面决定将采矿许可证再延长六年，违反了欧盟的规定。
边境另一边的当地积极分子担心矿井对地下水位、灰尘和噪音的影响。“水井正在干涸。随着矿井变得更深，离边界越来越近，更多的地下水流出。有些人--在21世纪--现在没有水了，“边境捷克一侧抗议采矿的居民米兰·斯塔雷克(Millan Starec)说。
PGE和波兰政府表示，计划中的采矿区在1994年最初的许可证规定的边界内。PGE表示，其研究表明对地下水位的影响微乎其微。为了防止排水，该公司还在工地上修建了地下屏障。
根据美国能源情报署(US Energy Information Administration)的数据，燃烧天然气排放的二氧化碳几乎是燃烧天然气的两倍，以产生相同数量的能源。
当波兰签署2015年巴黎协定时，它承诺将全球气温上升控制在远低于工业化前水平2摄氏度的水平。为了限制更多的变暖，世界需要迅速摆脱化石燃料。
根据研究机构气候分析(Climate Analytics)2017年的一项研究，如果欧盟(EU)，包括波兰，如果想要与《巴黎协定》(Paris Agreement)兼容，到2030年，它将需要将与煤炭相关的排放削减到接近于零的水平。
这项研究估计，图罗发电厂需要在2024年至2028年之间的某个时候关闭。超过这一日期将使未来的气候变暖更加难以控制。
但PGE决心将运营持续到2044年。该公司目前正在发电厂建造一个新机组，这是一个35亿兹罗提(9.3亿美元)的投资项目，只有在允许它按照计划的路线运行的情况下，这个项目才会变得经济。新装置计划于明年4月开始运营。
波兰强烈抵制迅速淘汰煤炭的呼吁。该国是唯一拒绝承诺到2050年实现碳中和的欧盟成员国。
波兰政府正在通过购买不需要的煤炭和禁止进口更便宜的煤炭来支撑日益不经济的煤炭行业。中国还一直在投资可再生能源，特别是海上风电场和太阳能。
副总理Jacek Sasin本月对波兰电视频道Polsat表示，虽然未来有必要逐步淘汰煤炭，但他预计煤矿开采要到“2050年至2060年”才会停止.。
波兰正在与欧盟发生冲突，欧盟本身在自己的一些气候目标上落后了。根据气候行动跟踪者(Climate Action Tracker)即将发布的一份政策分析，欧洲目前的政策仍然不符合“巴黎协定”(Paris Agreement)。这份分析报告独家与CNN分享。
根据这项新的研究，如果欧盟想要遵守该协议，它必须设定更雄心勃勃的近期目标。目前2030年的目标是在1990年的基础上减少至少40%的排放量，气候行动追踪机构称这一目标“严重不足”。
根据“巴黎协定”，各国在减排方面应以“尽可能高的抱负”为目标，但目前还没有关于每个国家在全球负担中的份额应该是多少的指导方针。
气候行动跟踪器根据每个国家的经济和历史情况，并以出版的科学文献为支持，计算了每个国家的数字。根据气候行动跟踪者(Climate Action Tracker)的说法，为了在全球努力中贡献自己的公平份额，欧盟的目标应该是到2030年将排放量比1990年的水平减少约65%。
“这是不够的，因为它是基于10年前的情况，”气候行动追踪研究的主要作者之一、新气候研究所的Niklas Höhne教授说。“过去10年的排放量比我们当时预想的要高得多。我们有了新的“巴黎协定”目标。“。
欧盟正试图协商一个更雄心勃勃的2030年目标，但达成协议将很艰难。
今年夏天，当欧盟同意提供2万亿美元的冠状病毒救援基金时，其最初目标是将现金的支付与气候中立承诺捆绑在一起。波兰成功地反对这一要求，将能够在不同意到2050年将排放减少到净零的情况下获得这笔资金。
欧盟仍将30%的资金专门用于气候变化，但它已经淡化了旨在防止任何剩余资金进入污染行业的保障措施。波兰尚未透露计划如何支出其份额。波兰政府没有回应CNN的置评请求，但本月早些时候公布的长期能源计划显示，波兰计划增加对可再生能源和核能的投资。
尽管波兰反对快速过渡，但它已经在经历气候变化的影响。气温上升，热浪和干旱变得更加普遍。
在图罗，几乎没有人看到气候变化和发电厂之间的联系。在过去的20年里，该建筑群采用了更严格的环境标准，对环境的明显影响没有以前那么严重。70年代和80年代在附近的吉泽拉山脉造成环境灾难的大部分有毒排放物都消失了。雪不再因为矿上漏出的细尘而变黑了。
当气候危机关系到他们的生计时，该地区的大多数人觉得他们不能担心气候危机。
“我不把我的工作和冬天雪少联系起来。这些事情每年都会改变。这些东西会自然而然地改变。我们，这个矿场，每年产生的排放量越来越少。我们开采的煤炭越来越少，“矿工普平说。
我真的不太明白这个问题。这一地区几个世纪以来一直是煤炭产区。
我大约20年前开始在这里工作。我一放学就受雇在这里工作。我弟弟在矿井里工作。他已经在那里工作了25年了。我父亲在矿井工作。在我们地区，全家在建筑群里工作的情况经常发生。
我真的不太明白这个问题。这一地区几个世纪以来一直是煤炭产区。德国和捷克的许多煤矿和发电厂都比我们的大，它们的运营没有出现这样的问题。
我感到自豪，我感到责任重大。工厂位于一个独特的位置，我们的活动确实会影响我们的邻居。我们在努力成为好邻居方面投入了大量资金。我们满足了最严格的要求，即使这些要求不是法律规定的。
当然，改变是必要的。我们必须推进能源转型。但这需要是可持续的，它们必须在很长一段时间内发生。我们需要一个适应的机会。
这真的是件大事。很多人在矿井和发电厂工作。薪水很高。对于经济来说，继续下去是很重要的。我认为(该地区)超过50%的人在那里工作。这其中有很大的联系。
我是一名理疗师和骨科医生。我的很多客户都在那里工作。很多人去德国工作，但工作量不大。这是为年轻人准备的工作。这个矿场是给老年人开的。有些人已经在那里工作了20年、30年、40年。
对我来说，浪费(比气候变化)是一个更大的问题。我们需要重新考虑如何处理废物。
如果该矿继续运营六年，也不会有任何改变。在25年内？我不知道会发生什么。不行。我们现在有一场大流行。什么事都有可能发生。
当我还是个孩子的时候，我们这里从来没有，从来没有过白雪。它是黑色的。因为灰尘，雪是黑色的。
因为气候变化，我现在会关闭所有的燃煤电厂。绝对一点儿没错。我们不必再破坏地球了，现在的技术使我们有可能转向更绿色的能源。
我出生在这里，我记得，当我还是个孩子的时候，我们这里从来没有，从来没有过白雪。它是黑色的。因为灰尘，雪是黑色的。现在，矿井没有这些问题。
我认识很多在矿井里工作的人。我知道我在这里是少数。但即使是在那里工作的人，他们也知道这只是个时间问题。
我在市文化中心工作。封锁对我们影响很大。经济也是如此。这是一个市政设施，所以如果税收下降，他们就不能资助我们了。通常，当经济低迷时，文化是第一个看到削减的。如果矿山关闭，该镇的经济将受到影响。我们肯定会感受到它的影响。但我希望政府能找到解决方案。我们为此缴税。
21年前，我们在Turów矿开始了我们的职业生涯。我的家族从第二次世界大战后就开始和这个矿场有联系。在这块土地被并入波兰之后，我的祖父就定居在这里的西部。
他在一家寻找煤矿的公司工作，一段时间后，他被煤矿雇佣为传送带操作员。我母亲作为一个20岁的女孩在矿井里找到了一份工作。她在1967年或1968年认识了我的父亲，他们都开始在矿井工作。
我为自己是一名矿工而感到自豪。我为我的传统感到自豪。我把自己和这个地方联系在一起。
我们在WałBrzych已经经历了矿业衰退，当那里的矿山匆忙关闭的时候，造成了什么样的后果。我们看到人们陷入贫困，社区退化，实际上花了20年，也许是30年，才让这座城市再次崛起。
国际社会要求关闭该矿的压力吓坏了当地居民。据PGE称，该建筑群约有5000名员工。该地区还有1万个工作岗位依赖于它。截至2018年，兹戈尔泽莱克地区劳动力总数为2.05万人，人口9万。
该地区市长arturbieliński说，如果煤矿和工厂突然关闭，那将是一场灾难。
“如果这么多人突然失业，会有很大的社会问题，”他说。“这种情况发生在20世纪90年代的WałBrzych，当时有四个矿场关闭了…。人们养成坏习惯、上瘾、酗酒，于是就有了犯罪。“。
Bieliński知道矿场最终会关闭。但他希望这一过程缓慢进行，并得到波兰政府和欧盟的财政帮助，他希望这能使该地区对投资具有吸引力。这是在整个地区重复的口头禅：“进化，而不是革命。”
欧盟已经拨出175亿欧元(2070万美元)用于其正义过渡基金，该基金旨在帮助那些在煤炭淘汰中蒙受损失的地区。欧盟委员会(European Commission)已经起草了一份根据该计划应该优先考虑的地区的入围名单，其中包括波兰的九个地区。兹戈尔泽莱克不是其中之一。据欧盟委员会称，这是因为只有明确承诺在不久的将来过渡的地区才有资格获得这笔资金，而图罗的扩张计划排除了这一可能性。
当1.3万捷克人签署了一份反对进一步采矿的请愿书并将其寄往欧盟时，波兰一侧矿场周围城镇的人们动员起来捍卫发电厂。他们收集了3万个签名，呼吁行动持续到2044年。
当地童子军组织，兹戈尔泽莱克市第69个游艇童子军组织“Przystan”的孩子们参与了这一努力。
15岁的桑德拉·贝泽克(Sandra Beczek)是参与收集签名的童子军之一。“我父母开了一家商店。而且很多人都在矿井里工作。如果他们失业了，他们就不会来商店了。所以该矿带动了这一地区的整个经济，“她说。
就像世界各地的童子军组织一样，“Przystan”参与旨在帮助环境的活动，包括清理当地的垃圾。但是他们的行动主义并没有延伸到瑞典气候活动家Greta Thunberg发起的那种学校罢工。
“我认为，因为我们支持矿山，并不意味着我们不支持环境。15岁的阿米莉亚·托卡斯卡(Amelia Tokarska)说，至于通伯格和她呼吁像波兰这样的国家立即关闭燃煤电厂呢？
托卡斯卡说：“她应该重新考虑一下，如果她立即关闭煤矿，会给社区带来什么后果。”“在这件事上，她并不孤单，她应该考虑其他人的生活，他们是如何受到影响的。”
肖恩·“大约翰”麦当劳在艾伯塔省的Kikino Metis定居点出生和长大，这是一块为当地原住民留出的土地。
这位49岁的企业主说，放学后，他直接进入了石油和天然气行业，“就像我父亲和艾伯塔省家庭、社区和其他社区中的许多人一样。”
对麦克唐纳来说，开发该地区的油砂是他祖先传统的合乎逻辑的延续。
他说：“收割权过去是通过狩猎驼鹿、诱捕和出售毛皮、采摘浆果和钓鱼等方式行使的。”“但随着时间的推移，石油和天然气行业进入了这个地区，我们开始为石油和天然气…工作。我们仍在收获我们的权利，但方式不同。“。
我很喜欢支持当地人。当像我这样的公司蓬勃发展时，整个当地都会有这种感觉。
他感觉很强壮。
.
note_en:
Covid-19 has devastated the global economy and forced governments around the world to pour trillions into the recovery effort.
 The pandemic could have been the decisive moment in the fight against climate change -- an opportunity for leaders to bail out the environment and pivot the planet toward a greener future.
 Instead, CNN has found that some of the biggest fossil fuel-producing countries are injecting taxpayer money into propping up polluting industries. And exclusive new data shows these decisions are taking the world a step closer to a  climate catastrophe.
 “This is the one chance that we have,” said professor Niklas Höhne, founding partner at the NewClimate Institute, a climate think tank, and co-author of an upcoming study from  the Climate Action Tracker shared with CNN.
 The research shows that the world is running well behind its already insufficient targets of limiting global warming to below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels and pursuing efforts to limit it to 1.5 degrees.
 “We are in a situation where enormous sums of money are (being) spent,” Höhne said. “A similar opportunity for spending so much money from government budgets will not come in the next 10 to 20 years.”
 Global greenhouse gas emissions have fallen due to coronavirus lockdowns. The current economic downturn means future emissions are projected to be slightly lower.
 But the recent decline won't have any real impact on long-term climate change, according to exclusive data from the Climate Action Tracker.
 The United Nations says it is necessary to phase out fossil fuels to stop catastrophic man-made climate change. But getting rid of them is going to be hard. Around the world, communities rely on fossil fuels for their energy, their jobs, their livelihoods. And, in turn, governments rely on their votes and taxes.
 Below, we look at four parts of the world once again turning to fossil fuels to help salvage their economies.
   Poland is spending $35 million to buy up unwanted coal to relieve an industry "shaken by the pandemic.” In Canada, the province of Alberta is investing $1.1 billion into a new oil pipeline, deemed "essential” for economic recovery. Australia’s Queensland is fast-tracking a new coal mine to help the state “bounce back from the impacts of Covid-19.” And India is opening up dozens of coal mines to the private sector to “turn the Covid-19 crisis into an opportunity.”
 These are the communities where the climate change battle is taking place -- and where people’s livelihoods depend on industries that the rest of the world needs them to give up.
  In this part of Poland, the local scouts campaign to keep their town's coal mine open
  Civil engineer Piotr Pupin has been working at the Turów lignite mine in southwestern Poland for the past 19 years. And just like his father did, he hopes to continue working there until it’s time to retire in a couple decades’ time.
 He’s got a problem. While the open pit mine holds enough coal to continue operations until 2044, its current permit is set to expire in just six years. And because of the climate crisis, there’s plenty of opposition in the European Union to further extensions.
 The 44-year old civil engineer grew up in the nearby town of Zgorzelec. While at university in Wroclaw, he won a scholarship paid for by the company that operated Turów at the time. In exchange for the funding, he signed a three-year contract to work in the mine. He stayed ever since, rising through the ranks to his current role as an investment supervisor. His team oversees investment into all of the infrastructure within the mine, from roads to buildings and electrical equipment to the huge machinery roaming the site.
 “I didn’t plan to stay for this long, but it so happened. My career was progressing, it was going well,” he said. “It’s not the job of my dreams, but it gives me comfort and stability and security.”
 In this region, most people work either in the mine complex or across the border.
  The potential shutdown of the mine and its affiliated power plant, run by the same company, would be disastrous for Pupin and thousands of others.
 “I’d have to search for a new job. Most likely, that would mean going across the borders,” he said. His skills are not limited to mining, but he doubts there would be work for him in the region.
 “All of the construction companies in this area, the companies which I could potentially work for with my qualifications, they all work for the mine. When the mining plant closes, these companies will not have a bright future.”
 Pupin knows the mine will shut eventually. Demand for coal is decreasing in Poland, being replaced by gas and renewable energy sources. Turów is already feeling the impact. “Every year, the number of workers at the mine goes down. People retire and new people are not hired to replace them,” he said. He knows his two daughters will likely move away when they grow up. The region is so dependent on the mine, it’s hard to come by a good job that’s not related to the site in one way or another.
 But he is hoping to stay until the end. “Two of my sisters left Poland for Ireland. That’s enough,” he said.
    Turów’s long-term future is more than uncertain. Located in a tiny, appendix-like tip of Poland, the mine complex is nestled between Germany and the Czech Republic, where it faces fierce opposition from environmental and climate activists as well as residents. Both countries have petitioned against the mine, even though they also rely on coal for power. According to the International Energy Agency, 80% of Poland’s domestic energy comes from coal, compared to 54% in the Czech Republic and 43% in Germany.
 Lignite, sometimes called brown coal due to its color, is the least efficient and most polluting type of coal. It has lower fuel value and higher emissions intensity compared to the geologically older hard coal that is mined underground. Lignite is difficult to transport, so it’s usually burnt for energy at the same place where it’s mined. In Turów, the freshly mined coal travels on conveyor belts to a power plant just some three kilometers away.
 The Polish government says Turów plays a strategic role in the country’s energy security. It supplies power to around 2.3 million Polish households, according to PGE, the state-controlled company that runs the mine. PGE is now planning to expand the digging closer towards the Czech border, a plan that has caused tension between the two long-time allies.
 The Czechs have  launched a complaint with the European Union, arguing that Poland breached EU rules when it unilaterally decided to extend the mining permit for further six years in March.
 Local activists across the border are worried about the mine’s impact on ground water levels, dust and noise. “The wells are drying out. As the mine becomes deeper and inches closer to the border, more ground water flows away. There are people who -- in the 21st century -- are now left without water,” said Milan Starec, one of the residents on the Czech side of the border protesting the mine.
 PGE and the Polish government say the planned mining area is within the boundaries stipulated in the original 1994 permit. PGE says its studies have shown a minimal impact on ground water levels. To prevent drainage, the company is also building an underground barrier on the site.
 Burning it emits almost twice as much carbon dioxide as burning natural gas to produce the same amount of energy, according to the US Energy Information Administration.
 When Poland signed the 2015 Paris Agreement, it pledged to keep global temperature rises to well below 2 degrees Celsius above pre-industrial levels. To limit more warming, the world needs to quickly transition away from fossil fuels.
 The EU, including Poland, will need to slash its coal-related emissions close to zero by 2030, if it wants to be compatible with the Paris Agreement,  according to a 2017 study by the research institute Climate Analytics.
 The study estimates the Turów power plant needs to shut down sometime between 2024 and 2028. Going beyond that date will make future warming more difficult to control.
 But PGE is set on keeping the operations going until 2044. It’s currently building a new unit at the power plant, a 3.5 billion Zloty ($930 million) investment project that will only become economical if it’s allowed to run its planned course. The new unit is scheduled to start operating in April next year.
   Poland is fiercely resisting the calls for a rapid phaseout of coal. The country is the only EU member state refusing to pledge carbon neutrality by 2050.
 The Polish government is propping up the increasingly uneconomical coal industry by buying up unwanted coal and banning imports of cheaper coal. The country has also been investing into renewables, especially offshore wind farms and solar.
 Deputy Prime Minister Jacek Sasin told the Polish TV channel Polsat this month that while phasing out coal will be necessary in the future, he doesn’t expect coal mining to stop until “between 2050 and 2060.”
 Poland is on a collision course with the EU, which is itself running behind on some of its own climate goals. According to a soon-to-be-published policy analysis by  the Climate Action Tracker, shared exclusively with CNN, Europe’s current policies are still not compatible with the Paris Agreement.
  The EU must set more ambitious near-term goals if it wants to comply with the accord, according to the new study. The current 2030 target is to cut emissions by at least 40% from 1990 levels, which the Climate Action Tracker says is “severely inadequate.”
 Under the Paris Agreement, countries should aim for the “highest possible ambition” when it comes to emissions reductions, but there are currently no guidelines on what each nation’s share of the global burden should be.
 The Climate Action Tracker has calculated country-by-country figures, based on each nation’s economic and historical circumstances and backed by published scientific literature. In order to contribute its fair share to global efforts, the EU should be aiming to cut its emissions by around 65% compared to 1990 levels by 2030, according to  the Climate Action Tracker.
 “It’s insufficient because it was based on the situation 10 years ago,” said professor Niklas Höhne of the NewClimate Institute, one of the lead authors of the Climate Action Tracker study. “The emissions in the last 10 years have gone up much more than we had thought at that point in time. And we have the new Paris Agreement goal.”
  The EU is trying to negotiate a more ambitious 2030 target, but reaching an agreement will be tough.
 When the bloc agreed a  $2 trillion coronavirus relief fund in the summer, its original aim was to tie the disbursement of the cash to the climate neutrality pledge. Poland successfully campaigned against that requirement and will be able to access the funds without agreeing to cut emissions to net zero by 2050.
 The EU has still earmarked 30% of the funds for climate, but it has watered down safeguards designed to prevent any of the rest of the money from going into polluting sectors. Poland has not yet revealed how it plans to spend its share. The Polish government has not responded to CNN’s requests for comment, but its long-term energy plan published earlier this month reveals it is planning an increased investment into renewables and nuclear power.
 Despite its opposition to quick transition, Poland is already experiencing the effects of climate change. Temperatures have increased and  heat   waves and droughts have become more common.
  Few in Turów see a connection between climate change and the power plant. The complex has adopted more stringent environmental standards in the past two decades and its visible impact on the environment is less severe than it used to be. Gone are most of the poisonous emissions that caused environmental disasters in the nearby Jizera Mountains in the 70s and 80s. The snow no longer turns black because of the fine dust escaping from the mine.
 Most people in the area feel like they can’t afford to worry about the climate crisis when it's their livelihoods that are at stake.
 “I don’t link my job with there being less snow in the winter. These things change every year. These things change naturally. We are, the mine is, producing fewer and fewer emissions each year. We are mining less coal,” mine worker Pupin said.
 I don’t really understand the problem. This region has been a coal region for many centuries.
 I started working here almost 20 years ago. I was employed here right after school. My brother works in the mine. He has been working there for 25 years. My father worked in the mine. It happens very often in our region that whole families work in the complex.
 I don’t really understand the problem. This region has been a coal region for many centuries. Many mines and power plants in Germany and the Czech Republic are bigger than ours and they operate without such problems.
 I feel proud and I feel responsible. The plant is located in a unique location, our activity does impact our neighbors. And we are investing a lot into the efforts to be good neighbors. We are meeting the most stringent requirements, even if they are not mandated by the law.
 For sure, changes are necessary. We must progress with the energy transition. But this need to be sustainable, they must take place over a long period of time. We need a chance to adapt.
    It’s a really big deal. A lot of people work at the mine and the power plant. It pays very well. It’s important it keeps going, for the economy. I think more than 50% of people [in the region] work there. There’s a lot of connection to it.
 I am a physiotherapist and osteopath. Many of my clients work there. A lot of people go to Germany for work, but there’s not a lot of work. It’s work for young people. The mine is for older people. Some have been working there 20, 30, 40 years.
 For me, waste is a bigger problem [than climate change]. We need to rethink how we deal with waste.
 If the mine stays open for six years, it changes nothing. In 25 years? I don’t know what will happen. No way. We have a pandemic now. Anything can happen.
  When I was a child, we never, never had white snow here. It was black. The snow was black because of the dust.
 I would close all of the coal power plants now, because of climate change. Absolutely. We don’t have to ruin the Earth anymore, there is technology now that makes it possible to switch to greener energy.
 I was born here and I remember, when I was a child, we never, never had white snow here. It was black. The snow was black because of the dust. Now, the mine doesn’t have these problems.
 I know many people who work in the mine. I know I am in the minority here. But even the people who work there, they know this is just a matter of time.
 I work in the municipal cultural center. The lockdown has impacted us a lot. And the economy too. This is a municipal facility, so if the tax revenues go down, they can’t fund us. Usually, when the economy is down, the culture is the first to see cuts. If the mine closes, the economy of the town will suffer. We would definitely feel the impact of it. But I hope that the government will find a solution. We pay taxes for that.
    We began our professional career at the Turów mine 21 years ago. My family has been connected with the mine since after World War II. My grandfather settled here in the west after this part of the land was annexed to Poland.
 He worked for a company searching for the coal deposits and after some time he was employed by the mine as a conveyor operator. My mother took up a job in the mine as a 20-year-old girl. She met my father in 1967 or 1968 and they both began working at the mine.
 I am proud to be a miner. I am proud of my heritage. I associate myself with this place.
 We already experienced [the mining decline] in Wałbrzych, when mines there were closed down in a rush, and what kind of consequences it caused. We saw people in poverty, community degradation, and actually it took 20 years, and maybe 30 years, until this city rose again.
  The international pressure for the mine to shut down is scaring the local residents. According to PGE, the complex employs around 5,000 people. The jobs of a further 10,000 in the region depend on it. As of 2018, the Zgorzelec region had a total labor force of 20,500 people and a population of 90,000.
 The region’s mayor Artur Bieliński said that if the mine and the plant shut down suddenly, it would be a disaster.
 “There would be big social problems if this many people got unemployed suddenly,” he said. “It has happened in the 1990s in Wałbrzych when four mines closed … people develop bad habits, addiction, alcoholism and there’s crime.”
  Bieliński knows the mine will eventually close. But he wants it to happen slowly and with financial help from the Polish government and the EU that he hopes could make the region attractive to investment. It’s the mantra that is repeated throughout the region: “Evolution, not revolution.”
 The EU has set aside €17.5 billion ($20.7 million) for its Just Transition Fund which is meant to help regions that stand to lose from the coal phase-out. The European Commission has already drafted a shortlist of areas that should be prioritized under the scheme, including nine regions in Poland. Zgorzelec is not one of them. According to the Commission, that’s because only regions with a clear commitment to a transition in the near future are eligible for the funds, and Turów’s expansion plans rule that out.
 When 13,000 Czechs signed a petition against further mining and sent it to the EU, people from the towns surrounding the mine on the Polish side mobilized in defense of the power plant. They collected 30,000 signatures calling for the operations to continue until 2044.
 Kids from the local scouts group, the 69th yachting scouts group “Przystan” in Zgorzelec, took part in the effort.
 Sandra Beczek, 15, is one of the scouts involved in collecting the signatures. “My parents run a shop. And many people are working in the mine. If they lose their jobs, they won’t come to the shop. So the mine drives the whole economy in this region,” she said.
  Just like scout groups around the world, “Przystan” takes part in activities designed to help the environment, including local rubbish cleanups. But their activism doesn’t extend to the kind of school strikes started by the Swedish climate activist Greta Thunberg.
 “I think that because we support the mine doesn’t mean that we don’t support the environment. It doesn’t exclude one another,” said Amelia Tokarska, 15. And as for Thunberg and her call for countries like Poland to shut down coal power plants immediately?
 “She should rethink what the consequences for the community would be if she closed down the mines right away,” Tokarska said. “That she is not alone in this and should think about other people’s lives, how they are affected.”
 Shawn “Big John” McDonald was born and raised on the Kikino Metis settlement in Alberta, a piece of land set aside for the local aboriginal population.
 He got into the oil and gas industry straight after school, “like my father did and so many people within the family and the community and other communities in Alberta,” the 49-year-old business owner said.
 For McDonald, developing the area’s oil sands is a logical continuation of his ancestors’ traditions.
 “The harvesting rights used to be exercised by hunting, you know, moose, deer and trapping and selling the furs and picking berries and fishing and all that stuff,” he said. “But over time, the oil and gas industry came into the area and we started getting into working for oil and gas … we are still harvesting our rights, but in a different way.”
 I am big on supporting local. When companies like mine flourish, the whole local area feels that.

title: 科维德给了世界解决气候危机的机会。我们要浪费它了
title_en: Covid gave world opportunity to fix the climate crisis. We’re about to waste it

note:
跳转导航跳跃搜索第19号鼹鼠板球行动(希伯来语：מבצעערצב-19‎，MivtzaʻArtzav Tsha-Esreh)是以色列空军在1982年黎巴嫩战争开始时对叙利亚目标发动的镇压敌方防空行动。这是历史上第一次西方装备的空军成功摧毁苏联建造的地对空导弹(SAM)网络。[3]它也成为第二次世界大战以来规模最大的空战之一[6]，也是朝鲜战争以来规模最大的空战之一。[7]结果是以色列取得了决定性的胜利，从而产生了俗称贝卡山谷土耳其射击。
赎罪日战争结束时，IAF开始了一项压制萨姆导弹的行动。以色列和叙利亚在黎巴嫩问题上日益紧张的局势在20世纪80年代初升级，最终叙利亚在贝卡山谷部署了SAM系统。1982年6月6日，以色列入侵黎巴嫩，在战争的第三天，以色列国防军和叙利亚军队之间的冲突仍在继续，以色列决定发动这次行动。
这场战斗持续了大约两个小时，涉及创新的战术和技术。到当天结束时，IAF已经摧毁了部署在贝卡山谷的30个SAM连中的29个，击落了82-86架敌机，自己的损失最小。这场战斗导致美国对以色列和叙利亚实施停火。
在1973年的赎罪日战争中，埃及拥有20个移动SA-6地空导弹系统，70个SA-2，65个SA-3，以及2500多个防空炮台和大约3000个SA-7。叙利亚又部署了34个SAM连。在“模型5”行动中，以色列空军在战斗初期仓促执行了一次企图摧毁戈兰高地附近的SAM炮台的尝试，但没有成功，给攻击部队带来了灾难性的后果。总体而言，在战争的前三天，IAF总共损失了50架飞机，出动飞机约1220架次，损失率为4%。SA-6、SA-7和ZSU-23-4火炮击中了以色列战前总共170架A-4天鹰战斗机中的53架和177架F-4幻影战斗机中的33架。因此，IAF发现很难向地面部队提供空中支援。10月14日，当埃及试图突破其萨姆连的射程时，它损失了28架飞机，而以色列则赢得了一场意义重大的陆战。1973年至1978年间，IAF进行了一项重大项目，试图找到应对SAM威胁的答案。[8]。
以色列在1973年战争中遭受的损失如此之大，以至于间接催生了美国的隐形飞机计划，即蓝色计划。美国估计，如果导弹问题得不到解决，即使是美国，在美苏冲突爆发后的两周内，也会遭受空军的耗尽。以色列在18天内损失了109架飞机。[9]。
1980年5月28日，IAF制导导弹在西顿附近摧毁了两辆载有SA-9炮台的装甲车，由利比亚军队驾驶。[10]以色列媒体宣称找到了解决地对空导弹问题的办法，但以色列空军指挥官大卫·伊弗里说，这一评估为时过早，SA-9实际上并不比它的前身好很多。时任国防部长的总理梅纳赫姆·贝京宣布，IAF可以在两小时内摧毁SAM电池。艾弗里告诉媒体，IAF做不到这样的事情。[11]。
1981年4月28日，以色列空军(拉马特·大卫空军基地117个中队的F-16A战斗机)在黎巴嫩上空击落了两架叙利亚直升机。叙利亚的回应是向贝卡山谷部署了第一个萨姆旅。萨姆炮台并不是对以色列的直接战略威胁，而且在边界另一边的黎巴嫩东部已经有几个叙利亚的萨姆炮台。贝京面临两难境地：一方面，新的部署损害了以色列的威慑信誉，另一方面，打击可能会导致与叙利亚发生不必要的冲突。最终，他决定发动攻击，将于4月30日发动，但由于天气状况，行动被取消。[12]当天气放晴时，IAF正全神贯注于歌剧行动的准备工作。[8]与此同时，美国担心苏联对以色列袭击的反应可能会导致两个超级大国之间的危机，于是施压开始不发动袭击。以色列同意取消袭击，美国特使菲利普·哈比卜(Philip Habib)被派往该地区担任调解人。他往返于耶路撒冷和大马士革之间，但未能取下SAM电池。[13]。
12月14日，以色列通过了“戈兰高地法”，将戈兰高地并入以色列。叙利亚总统哈菲兹·阿萨德(Hafez al-Assad)认为这是宣战，但认为叙利亚没有条件进行战斗。[14]这项法律使以色列受到美国和国际社会的严厉批评。12月20日，以色列内阁召开了每周一次的会议，国防部长阿里尔·沙尔(Ariel Shar)在会上
打击地对空导弹的行动最初被称为“鼹鼠3”，但随着探测到的地对空导弹电池数量的增加，这个数字增加了，最终达到19个。此时，为了从心理上为部队做好准备，该行动更名为“鼹鼠板球”，这是1973年以来的一场全面战争的计划的名称的名字。(编者注：原文为“Mole Cricket”，原名为“Mole Cricket”，原名为“Mole Cricket”，即1973年以来的全面战争计划名称)。这个名字最早是在2002年披露的。[8]。
1982年6月8日星期二，当以色列军队向杰辛推进时，贝京在以色列议会向叙利亚人发表了他的部分声明，敦促他们不要干预战争，并除其他外说：我再次声明，我们不希望与叙利亚开战。从这个平台上，我呼吁阿萨德总统指示叙利亚军队不要伤害以色列士兵，这样(叙利亚士兵)就不会发生什么坏事。我们不希望与叙利亚军队发生冲突，如果我们到达距离我们北部边境40公里的防线，工作就已经完成，所有战斗都将结束。我要把我的话传给叙利亚总统听。他知道如何遵守协议。他与我们签署了停火协议，并遵守了协议。他不允许恐怖分子采取行动。如果他现在在黎巴嫩以这种方式行事，我们的士兵不会伤害任何叙利亚士兵。[16]。
那天晚上，正当黎巴嫩中部的杰辛战役肆虐时，沙龙在北部司令部的一次会议上表示，我们今天知道，将与叙利亚人发生直接对抗，并认为最好是深入黎巴嫩。[17]晚上11点，以色列国防军进入Ein Zahalta，陷入与叙利亚师的战斗，该师阻止了其前进。沙龙利用这一点作为他发起Mole Cricket 19的主要理由。[18]前副参谋长、摩萨德指定头目耶库蒂埃尔·亚当表示，他担心内阁没有完全意识到这场战争的范围和目标。[18]前副参谋长和摩萨德被指定的首领叶库蒂埃尔·亚当表示，他担心内阁没有完全意识到这场战争的范围和目标。尽管如此，沙龙还是允许北方司令部司令阿米尔·德罗里(Amir Drori)驾车进入贝卡山谷，副总参谋长摩西·利维(Mohe Levi)飞往Ein Zahalta告诉指挥官梅纳赫姆·艾南，以色列空军当天将攻击叙利亚导弹。[19]与此同时，艾弗里了解到，无人驾驶飞行器又发现5架SA-6从戈兰高地进入贝卡山谷。IAF将这一举动解读为叙利亚无意卷入一场重大战争的信号-否则萨姆导弹就会处于捍卫接近大马士革的立场。重新部署向艾弗里表明，他们可以在不冒与叙利亚爆发全面战争的风险的情况下打击萨姆地点。[2]。
沙龙飞回耶路撒冷参加6月9日星期三的内阁会议，他说叙利亚已经开始将其第二装甲师(第三装甲师)从Ein Zahalta向南移动。政治时间表规定，等到周四将没有足够的时间进行地面推进。与沙龙一同出席会议的德罗里反对这次攻击，称这是多余的。埃坦态度矛盾，更愿意将袭击限制在向叙利亚人发出的信号上。交通部长莫迪凯·齐波里(Mordechai Tzipori)辩称，沙龙的计划已经超过了原来的40公里线。沙龙回答说，这条线必须从以色列最北端的梅图拉开始测量。他补充说，为了拯救Ein Zahalta的部队，有必要摧毁Beqaa的导弹电池。[20]内政部长优素福·伯格(Yosef Burg)指出，与内阁希望的相反，针对叙利亚人的战斗似乎迫在眉睫，攻击导弹只会加剧局势，导致全面战争。沙龙强调了战场上士兵的脆弱性。贝京转向艾弗里的副手阿莫斯·阿米尔(Amos Amir)，要求他对IAF在这样一次袭击中的损失进行预测。阿米尔回答说，我不能保证不会有任何损失，但损失会很小。贝京支持这次攻击，最终伯格也被说服了。沙龙离开了会议，并发布了空袭和地面行动的命令。[21]。
6月9日上午，以色列空军的飞机在黎巴嫩上空以三个级别运行。Kfis和Skyhawks沿着从西顿到贝鲁特郊区的海岸线，为地面部队提供近距离空中支援，并打击巴勒斯坦解放组织的目标。在超过10,000英尺(3,000米)的第二层，类似的编队正在盘旋，等待命令。在最高层，鹰眼驻扎在那里，以确保空中管制。特遣部队的第一架飞机袭击了Jebel Baruk顶部的叙利亚雷达，该雷达当时控制着一大片区域。[22]。
艾弗里在上午10点收到了执行行动的绿灯，但到那时他已经将袭击推迟到下午2点。[2]下午1：30，伊坦命令伊夫里发动攻击，以色列飞机成对起飞。[23]第一波由96架F-15和F-16组成。第二波：
Mastiff RPV首先进入，让叙利亚的SAM打开雷达，让叙利亚人相信头顶上有许多攻击机。一旦叙利亚雷达追踪到獒，追踪信号就会传递给导弹射程之外的另一名侦察员。侦察员随后将信号转播给在海岸外轨道飞行的E2C鹰眼飞机。收集的数据由E2CS和波音707 ECM飞机分析。[25]一旦地空导弹机组人员向无人机发射导弹，F-15和F-16提供空中掩护，而F-4幽灵攻击地空导弹连，用AGM-78和AGM-45反辐射导弹摧毁它们。[27]导弹的快速飞行时间将F-4S&39；暴露在地对空导弹中的风险降至最低。[2]据报道，叙利亚人发射了57架SA-6，但没有效果。[30][30]。
根据艾弗里的说法，许多萨姆炮台都在叙利亚边境一侧。[3]埃坦说，从作战角度看，我可以说，早在战争之前，我们就使用了迷你RPV来识别和定位所有叙利亚导弹炮台。然后，我们使用了高级电子设备，使我们能够使导弹发射场失明或失效。我们使他们无法对我们的飞机在高空进行可靠的修理。但在直接空袭之前，我们使用了远程火炮。[31][31]。
叙利亚人的回应是发射了大约100架战斗机来阻止袭击。[3]拦截IAF飞行员经常依赖甚高频无线电，希望保持他们的战术通信和与指挥所的联系。选择性机载通信干扰扰乱了米格-21和米格-23的无线电波，切断了它们与地面控制的联系，使它们容易受到来自以色列F-15和F-16的预警机的攻击。[2][27]。
IAF在叙利亚的三个主要机场部署了RPV，以报告叙利亚飞机的起飞时间和数量。数据被传输到E-2C。IAF利用米格只有机头和机尾警报雷达系统，而没有侧面警告或仰视和俯视系统的事实，通过干扰GCI通信网来实现这一点。E-2C引导以色列飞机就位，使他们能够从侧面攻击叙利亚飞机，而叙利亚飞机在那里不会有任何警告。由于干扰，叙利亚GCI管制员无法将飞行员引向即将到来的以色列飞机。[32]麻雀导弹的攻击速度为3.5马赫，射程为22至40公里(14至25英里)，这意味着它们不仅超出了叙利亚人的雷达范围，也超出了他们的可视范围。响尾蛇近距离迎面攻击能力给了以色列火力优势。[33][33]。
在谈到叙利亚对袭击的反应时，埃坦说：当我们袭击叙利亚人的导弹时，他们的第一反应是紧急派遣他们的空军……。任何(叙利亚战斗机飞行员)向我们的部队方向越过想象中的线，都会被摧毁，被击落。这条虚构的线实际上是叙利亚境内导弹炮台的射程。叙利亚空军的基本战术是升空并越过这条假想的线，这将他们带到了他们本土导弹的保护范围之外。他们做了力所能及的事，然后跑回去找掩护。
下午4点左右，14个电池被摧毁，离天黑还有一个小时，艾弗里决定取消行动，假设已经取得了最佳结果，叙利亚人将在第二天将更多的地对空导弹部署到位。下午4点刚过，操作就停止了。行动参谋人员Aviem Sella上校后来说，沙龙严厉批评了这一决定。[8]。
当晚，以色列空军摧毁了叙利亚陆军在巴勒贝克以北向南移动的第47装甲旅，第二天，以色列空军摧毁了另外6个萨姆连，其中2个是行动中遗留下来的，4个是叙利亚人当晚进入贝卡山谷的。[35]叙利亚国防部长穆斯塔法·特拉斯(Mustafa Tlass)告诉阿萨德，叙利亚空军已经落后，地对空导弹毫无用处，没有空中掩护，军队无法继续战斗。[27][27]。
沙龙后来说，如果我们容忍这种发展，叙利亚装甲部队将由600辆坦克组成，并受到广泛的导弹保护伞的保护。他们的导弹炮台向我们的飞机开火。我们别无选择，只能批准采取军事行动摧毁导弹集结。他把这次行动称为入侵的转折点。[36]一名被广泛认为是伊夫里本人的高级空军军官后来表示，叙利亚飞机处于劣势，不得不在对叙利亚不利的总体战略和战术形势下，无论何时何地对以色列的威胁做出回应。
齐波里后来在他的书中写道，沙龙从战争前夕就开始策划袭击，但欺骗内阁相信与叙利亚人的对抗是出乎意料的。沙龙坚称，他已在6月6日命令以色列国防军不要越过阿瓦利河，并避免与叙利亚人发生对抗。沙龙说，他确实命令军队准备应急计划，然而，在贝鲁特-大马士革高速公路上行驶，以防叙利亚人首先发动袭击。[38]伊坦声称，在行动的前一天晚上，他与沙龙达成一致，如果内阁批准，他将为以色列空军的袭击做好准备。[39]以色列高级指挥官在行动结束后说，加利利和平行动本来可以在不与叙利亚人对抗的情况下实现。[40]。
周三，阿萨德派遣特拉斯前往莫斯科，寻求全面的空中保护伞。苏联拒绝了，但在机场准备了大量军事装备，准备派往叙利亚，并派遣帕维尔·库塔霍夫元帅(Marshal Pavel Kutakhov)前往叙利亚，了解叙利亚的萨姆导弹发生了什么，担心北约可能会在东欧采取同样的行动。6月9日，阿萨德在大马士革会见了美国特使哈比卜，拒绝了他的条件，要求以色列从黎巴嫩撤军，作为停火的条件。美国总统罗纳德·里根(Ronald Reagan)呼吁贝京和阿萨德接受6月10日早上6点生效的停火协议。截至周五中午停火生效时，空军已经击落了82架飞机，没有一架飞机在空战中丧生。[2]战斗一年后，由时任负责计划和行动的副参谋长约翰·查克中将率领的美国实况调查团抵达以色列，以便从这场战斗中吸取教训。[42]。
苏联军事报纸Krasnaya Zvezda宣布，67架以色列飞机，包括现代的美国制造的F-15和F-16战斗机，在战斗中被击落。该报还报道了与一名叙利亚飞行员的会面，这名飞行员讲述了他在一次交战中击落一架以色列F-15：&#34；胜利并不容易，敌人是微妙的。即使在苏联内部，这些说法也遭到了极大的质疑。[27]1991年，艾弗里遇到了一位1982年在莫斯科服役的捷克将军。他告诉艾弗里，这次行动让苏联人明白，西方的技术要优于他们的技术，在他看来，对贝卡山谷导弹系统的打击是对格拉斯诺斯特和苏联解体的推动。[2][8]。

Jump to navigation  Jump to search  Operation Mole Cricket 19 ( Hebrew:  מבצע ערצב-19‎,  Mivtza ʻArtzav Tsha-Esreh) was a  suppression of enemy air defenses (SEAD) campaign launched by the  Israeli Air Force (IAF) against  Syrian targets on June 9, 1982, at the outset of the  1982 Lebanon War. The operation was the first time in history that a Western-equipped air force successfully destroyed a  Soviet-built  surface-to-air missile (SAM) network.  [3] It also became one of the biggest air battles since  World War II,  [6] and the biggest since the  Korean War.  [7] The result was a decisive Israeli victory, leading to the colloquial name the "Bekaa Valley Turkey Shoot".
 The IAF began working on a SAM suppression operation at the end of the  Yom Kippur War. Rising tensions between Israel and Syria over Lebanon escalated in the early 1980s and culminated in Syria deploying SAM batteries in the  Beqaa Valley. On June 6, 1982, Israel invaded Lebanon, and on the third day of the war, with clashes ongoing between the  Israel Defense Forces (IDF) and the  Syrian Army, Israel decided to launch the operation.
 The battle lasted about two hours, and involved innovative tactics and technology. By the end of the day, the IAF had destroyed 29 of 30 SAM batteries deployed in the Beqaa Valley, and shot down 82–86 enemy aircraft, with minimal losses of its own. The battle led the  United States to impose a ceasefire on  Israel and  Syria.
    In the  Yom Kippur War of 1973,  Egypt had 20 mobile  SA-6 SAM systems, backed up by 70  SA-2s, 65  SA-3s, and upward of 2,500 anti-aircraft batteries and about 3,000  SA-7s. Syria deployed another 34 SAM batteries.  Operation Model 5, a hastily-executed IAF attempt to take out the SAM batteries near the Golan Heights early in the fighting was unsuccessful, with catastrophic results for the attacking force. In total, during the first three days of the war, the IAF lost 50 aircraft in about 1,220 sorties, a loss rate of four percent. The SA-6s, SA-7s, and  ZSU-23-4 guns hit 53 of Israel's prewar total of 170  A-4 Skyhawks and 33 of its 177  F-4 Phantoms. As a result, the IAF found it difficult to provide air support to the ground troops. When Egypt tried to push beyond the range of its SAM batteries on October 14, it lost 28 aircraft while Israel won a  significant land battle. Between 1973 and 1978, the IAF undertook a major project to try to find an answer to the SAM threat.  [8]
 The losses suffered by Israel in the 1973 war were so high that it indirectly spawned the United States stealth aircraft program, Project  HAVE BLUE. The U.S. estimated that without a solution to the SAM problem, even the United States would suffer depletion of its Air Force within two weeks of a conflict erupting between the U.S. and Soviet Union. The Israelis had lost 109 aircraft in 18 days.  [9]
 On May 28, 1980, IAF guided missiles destroyed two armored cars carrying  SA-9 batteries, manned by  Libyan troops, near  Sidon.  [10] The Israeli media declared that a solution to the SAM problem was found, but the IAF Commander,  David Ivry, said that this assessment was premature, and that the SA-9 was not really significantly superior to its predecessors. The  Prime Minister,  Menachem Begin, then also  Minister of Defense, announced that the IAF could destroy the SAM batteries in two hours. Ivry told the media that the IAF could do nothing of the kind.  [11]
  On April 28, 1981 the IAF (F-16A fighters from 117 squadron in Ramat David air base) shot down two Syrian helicopters over Lebanon. Syria responded by deploying its first SAM brigades to the Beqaa Valley. The SAM batteries were not a direct strategic threat to Israel, and there were already several Syrian SAM batteries in east Lebanon, across the border. Begin faced a dilemma: on one hand, the new deployment damaged Israel's deterrence credibility and, on the other hand, a strike might lead to an unnecessary clash with Syria. Eventually, he decided on an attack, to be launched on April 30, but the operation was called off due to weather conditions.  [12] By the time the weather cleared, the IAF was preoccupied with preparations for  Operation Opera.  [8] Meanwhile, the United States was concerned that Soviet reaction to an Israeli strike might lead to a crisis between the superpowers, and pressured Begin not to attack. Israel agreed to cancel the strike, and an American envoy,  Philip Habib, was sent to the region as a mediator. He shuttled between  Jerusalem and  Damascus but failed to get the SAM batteries removed.  [13]
 On December 14, Israel passed the  Golan Heights Law which annexed the  Golan Heights to Israel. The  Syrian President,  Hafez al-Assad, considered it a declaration of war, but believed that Syria was in no condition to fight it.  [14] The law subjected Israel to severe American and international criticism. On December 20, the  Cabinet of Israel convened for a weekly meeting, in which  Defense Minister  Ariel Sharon and the Chief of Staff ( Ramatkal)  Rafael Eitan presented the "Big Plan" for an invasion of Lebanon, which included seizure of the Beirut-Damascus Highway. Begin supported the plan, but other members of the Cabinet opposed and he decided to cancel it.  [15]
 The SAM suppression operation was originally called "Mole 3", but the figure increased in respect to the number of SAM batteries detected, eventually reaching 19. At this point the name was changed to "Mole Cricket", after the name of the plan for a general war since 1973, in order to prepare the force psychologically. The name was first revealed in 2002.  [8]
  On Tuesday June 8, 1982, while Israeli forces were advancing on  Jezzine, Begin addressed part of his statement in the  Knesset to the Syrians, urging them not to intervene in the war and saying,  inter alia, that: "I once again state that we do not want a war with Syria. From this platform I call on President Assad to instruct the Syrian army not to harm Israeli soldiers and then nothing bad will happen to [Syrian soldiers]. We desire no clashes with the Syrian army, if we reach the line 40 km from our northern border the work will have been done, all fighting will end. I am directing my words to the ears of the President of Syria. He knows how to keep an agreement. He signed a cease-fire with us and kept it. He did not allow the terrorists to act. If he behaves in this manner now in Lebanon, no Syrian soldier will be harmed by our soldiers".  [16]
  That night, while the  Battle of Jezzine was raging in central Lebanon, Sharon said at a meeting in the  Northern Command headquarters "We know today that there will be a direct confrontation with the Syrians" and argued that it would be best to move deeper into Lebanon.  [17] At 11:00 PM the IDF entered  Ein Zahalta and were caught in a battle against a Syrian division, which halted its advance. Sharon used this as his principal argument for launching Mole Cricket 19.  [18]  Yekutiel Adam, former Deputy Chief of Staff and designated Head of the  Mossad, voiced his concern that the Cabinet was not fully aware of the war's scope and objective. Nevertheless, Sharon gave North Command chief,  Amir Drori, a go-ahead to drive on the Beqaa valley, and Deputy Chief of Staff  Moshe Levi flew to Ein Zahalta to tell the commander,  Menachem Einan, that the IAF would attack the Syrian missiles that day.  [19] Meanwhile, Ivry learned that  unmanned aerial vehicles had spotted an additional five SA-6s moving from the  Golan Heights into the Beqaa Valley. The IAF interpreted that move as a signal that Syria had no intention of becoming involved in a major war—otherwise the SAMs would have been positioned to defend the approach to  Damascus. The redeployment suggested to Ivry that they could strike the SAM sites without risking an all-out war with Syria.  [2]
  Sharon flew back to  Jerusalem to attend a Cabinet meeting on Wednesday, June 9. He said Syria had already begun moving its second armored division (the  3rd Armoured Division) south from Ein Zahalta. The political timetable dictated that waiting until Thursday would not leave enough time for a ground advance. Drori, who came with Sharon to the meeting, was against the attack, saying it was superfluous. Eitan was ambivalent, preferring to limit the attack to a signal to the Syrians. The  Communications Minister,  Mordechai Tzipori, argued that Sharon's plan had exceeded the original forty-kilometer line. Sharon replied that the line had to be measured from Israel's northernmost point,  Metula. He added that destroying the missile batteries in the Beqaa was necessary to save the force at Ein Zahalta.  [20] The  Interior Minister,  Yosef Burg, pointed out that a fight against the Syrians seemed imminent, in contrast to what the Cabinet wanted, and that attacking the missiles would only aggravate things and lead to an all-out war. Sharon stressed the vulnerability of the soldiers in the field. Begin turned to Ivry's deputy,  Amos Amir, and asked for his projection of the IAF's losses in such an attack. Amir replied "I cannot promise no losses whatsoever, but they will be minimal". Begin supported the attack and eventually Burg was also convinced. Sharon left the conference and issued an order for an air strike as well as a ground operation.  [21]
   On the morning of June 9, the IAF aircraft over Lebanon were operating at three levels.  Kfirs and  Skyhawks were along the coastline from  Sidon to the outskirts of  Beirut, providing close air support for the ground troops and striking at  Palestine Liberation Organization targets. At the second level, over 10,000 feet (3,000 m), similar formations were circling and awaiting orders. At the top level, Hawkeyes were stationed to ensure air control. The first task force aircraft attacked the Syrian radar at the top of  Jebel Baruk, which was commanding a large area.  [22]
 Ivry received the green light at 10:00 AM to execute the operation, but by then he had postponed the attack until 2:00 PM.  [2] At 1:30 PM, Eitan ordered Ivry to strike, and the Israeli aircraft took off in pairs.  [23] The first wave was made up of 96  F-15s and  F-16s. The second wave which attacked the SAM batteries at 3:50 PM was made up of 92 aircraft. When the attack was launched, the Syrians ordered their combat air patrols to return to base and land.  [24]
 IAF aircraft carried electronic countermeasures pods to disrupt radar tracking. The IAF command post in  Tel Aviv provided Ivry a real-time command picture of the air battle through various data links.  E-2Cs with airborne surveillance radar down-linked their pictures to the command post. A squadron of  Tadiran Mastiff and  IAI Scout  Remotely Piloted Vehicles (RPV) kept at least two vehicles in the air all the time, providing constant location of the SAM batteries. A two-way voice communications link between Ivry and his pilots was set up, allowing for real-time command.  [2]  [25]
  The primary Syrian fighters involved were  MiG-21, with considerable numbers of  MiG-23s and  Su-20s also deployed. Syrian aircraft depended on  ground-controlled interception (GCI) sites for command and control.  [26] The SAM sites were a combination of  SA-2s,  SA-3s, and  SA-6s.  [2] The IAF's F-15s,  F-16s,  F-4s, and  Kfirs were equipped with  AIM-7F Sparrow radar-guided missiles,  AIM-9L Sidewinder infrared-guided missiles, and computer-aimed 20-mm cannons.  [27] The F-15s and  F-16s were equipped with a  head-up display (HUD) system.  [28]
 The Mastiff RPVs went in first to cause the Syrian SAMs to turn on their radars by convincing the Syrians that many attack aircraft were overhead. Once the Mastiffs were tracked by Syrian radar, the tracking signals were relayed to another Scout outside of the missiles' range. The Scout then relayed the signal to E2C Hawkeye aircraft orbiting off the coast. The data gathered was analyzed by the E2Cs and  Boeing 707 ECM aircraft.  [25] Once the SAM crews fired missiles at the drones, the F-15s and F-16s provided air cover while F-4 Phantoms attacked the SAM batteries, destroying them with  AGM-78 and  AGM-45  anti-radiation missiles.  [27]  [29] The rapid flight time of the missiles minimized the F-4s' exposure to the SAMs.  [2] The Syrians reportedly fired 57 SA-6s, to no effect.  [30]
 According to Ivry, many of the SAM batteries were on the Syrian side of the border.  [3] Said Eitan, "From the operational point of view I can say that we used the mini-RPVs, long before the war, to identify and locate all the Syrian missile batteries. We then used superior electronic devices which enabled us to "blind" or neutralize the missile sites' ground-to-air radar. We rendered them ineffective to take reliable fixes on our aircraft aloft. But in advance of direct aerial attacks, we used long-range artillery".  [31]
  The Syrians responded by launching about 100 fighter aircraft to stop the attacks.  [3] Intercepting IAF pilots relied frequently on  VHF radio, in hopes of preserving their tactical communications and links to the command post. Selective airborne communications jamming disrupted the airwaves for the MiG-21s and MiG-23s and cut them off from ground control, making them vulnerable to  AWACS-directed attacks from the Israeli F-15s and F-16s.  [2]  [27]
 The IAF positioned RPVs over three major airfields in Syria to report when and how many Syrian aircraft were taking off. The data was transmitted to the E-2Cs. The IAF took advantage of the fact that the MiGs had only nose and tail alert radar systems and no side warnings or look-up and look-down systems, by jamming the GCI communications net. E-2Cs guided the Israeli aircraft into positions that enabled them to attack the Syrian aircraft from the side, where the latter would have no warning. Because of the jamming, the Syrians GCI controllers could not direct their pilots toward the incoming Israeli aircraft.  [32] The Sparrow missiles attacked at speeds of Mach 3.5 at ranges of 22 to 40 km (14 to 25 miles), which meant that they were not only outside the Syrians' radar range but also outside their visual range. The Sidewinders' "head-on" capabilities at close range gave the Israelis firepower advantage.  [33]
 Discussing the Syrian response to the attacks, Eitan said: "The first reaction of the Syrians when we attacked their missiles was to scramble their air forces... any [Syrian fighter pilots] who crossed an imaginary line in the direction of our forces was destroyed, shot down. The imaginary line was actually the range of the missile batteries in Syria proper. The basic tactic of the Syrian air force is to take to the air and to cross this imaginary line, which brings them outside the protective range of their home-based missiles. They do what they can, then run back for cover."  [34]
 Near 4:00 PM, with fourteen batteries destroyed and an hour left until dark, Ivry decided to call off the operation, assuming the optimal result had been achieved and that the Syrians would move more SAMs into place the next day. The operation was stopped shortly after 4:00 PM. Colonel  Aviem Sella, a member of the operation staff, later said that Sharon severely criticized that decision.  [8]
   That night, the IAF destroyed the  Syrian Army's 47th Armored Brigade north of  Baalbek as it was moving south, and the following day, the IAF destroyed six other SAM batteries, two that remained from the operation and four that the Syrians moved into the Beqaa valley that night.  [35] Syrian Defense Minister  Mustafa Tlass told Assad that "the Syrian Air Force was outclassed, the ground-to-air missiles useless, and that without air cover, the army could not fight on".  [27]
 Sharon later said that "If we had tolerated that development, the Syrian armored forces would have consisted of 600 tanks protected by an extensive missile umbrella. Their missile batteries fired at our planes. We had no choice other than to approve a military operation to destroy the missile buildup". He called the operation "the turning point" in the invasion.  [36] A senior IAF officer, widely believed to be Ivry himself, later said that "Syrian aircraft were fighting from a disadvantage, having to respond to the Israeli threat wherever and whenever it materialized, within a general strategic and tactical situation not in Syria's favor."  [37]
  Tzipori later wrote in his book that Sharon had been planning the attack since the eve of the war, but tricked the Cabinet into believing that the confrontation with the Syrians was unexpected. Sharon maintained that on June 6 he had ordered the IDF not to cross the  Awali River and to avoid a confrontation with the Syrians. Sharon said he did order the army to prepare for a contingency plan, however, to drive on the Beirut-Damascus highway in case the Syrians attacked first.  [38] Eitan claimed that on the night before the operation, he had agreed with Sharon to prepare the IAF for the attack, in case the Cabinet approved it.  [39] Senior Israeli commanders said after the operation that  Operation Peace for Galilee could have been achieved without confronting the Syrians.  [40]
 On Wednesday, Assad sent Tlass to Moscow to seek a comprehensive air umbrella. The  Soviets refused but prepared large amounts of military equipment at airfields for dispatch to Syria and sent Marshal  Pavel Kutakhov to Syria to find out what happened to the Syrian SAMs, fearing that  NATO might do the same in  Eastern Europe. On June 9, Assad met with American envoy Habib in  Damascus and rejected his terms, demanding that Israel withdraw its forces from Lebanon as a condition for a ceasefire.  US President  Ronald Reagan called on Begin and Assad to accept a ceasefire effective at 6:00 AM, on June 10.  [41] By noon on Friday, when the ceasefire took effect, the IAF had shot down 82 aircraft without losing any in air combat.  [2] A year after the battle, a US fact-finding mission headed by Lieutenant-General  John Chain, then deputy chief of staff for plans and operations, arrived in Israel to learn lessons from the battle.  [42]
 The Soviet military newspaper   Krasnaya Zvezda announced that "sixty-seven Israeli aircraft, including modern US-made F-15 and F-16 fighters, were downed" in the fighting. The newspaper also reported a meeting with a Syrian airman who recounted an engagement in which he shot down an Israeli F-15: "The victory had not been easy; the enemy had been subtle". Even within Soviet ranks, these claims met with great skepticism.  [27] In 1991, Ivry met a  Czech general who had been serving in Moscow in 1982. He told Ivry that the operation made the Soviets understand that Western technology was superior to theirs, and that in his view, the blow to the Beqaa Valley SAMs was an impetus to  Glasnost and the  Soviet Union's collapse.  [2]  [8]
